---
title: "Chapter 07: Difference-in-Differences"
description: "Detailed study notes for Chapter 7 of Impact Evaluation in Practice."
book_id: "impact-evaluation-in-practice"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Chapter"
chapter_number: 7
source_pages: "159-171"
citation_key: "gertler_martinez_premand_rawlings_vermeersch_2016_impact_evaluation_in_practice"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:ch07-chapter-7-difference-in-differences:c0001"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:evaluating-a-program-when-the-rule-of-assignment-is-less-clear:c0001"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:the-equal-trends-assumption-in-difference-in-differences:c0001"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:the-equal-trends-assumption-in-difference-in-differences:c0002"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2016</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Chapter 7</span><span>Source pages 159-171</span></div>

This note summarises Chapter 7, "Difference-in-Differences," from Paul J. Gertler, Sebastian
Martinez, Patrick Premand, Laura B. Rawlings, and Christel M. J. Vermeersch's *Impact Evaluation in
Practice* [@gertler_martinez_premand_rawlings_vermeersch_2016_impact_evaluation_in_practice]. It is
a paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a
substitute for the chapter.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source records at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the chapter's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, checklists, figures, boxes, or extended passages.

## Core Argument

Difference-in-differences estimates impact by comparing outcome changes over time between treatment and comparison groups, relying on the assumption that their trends would otherwise have moved together.

## Study Summary

Chapter 7 introduces difference-in-differences for settings where assignment rules are less clear
and evaluators have data before and after program exposure.

The design subtracts baseline differences between groups and then compares changes over time. This
can address time-invariant differences, but it cannot fix time-varying shocks that affect one group
differently.

The key identifying assumption is parallel or equal trends: without the program, treatment and
comparison outcomes would have moved together. The chapter shows why violations of that assumption
bias estimates.

The method is practical in many policy settings, especially when randomization is unavailable, but
it requires careful selection of comparison groups, pretrend checks where possible, and attention to
other simultaneous changes.

For close reading, track how the chapter uses difference-in-differences, parallel trends, comparison
group, counterfactual while moving through before-after logic, treatment and comparison changes,
equal trends assumption. The notes identify the design problem the chapter helps solve and what
should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the methods sequence.

## Key Concepts

- **difference-in-differences**: A quasi-experimental method that compares changes over time in treatment and comparison groups.
- **parallel trends**: The difference-in-differences assumption that treatment and comparison outcomes would have moved together without the program.
- **comparison group**: Units used to estimate what would have happened to the treatment group without the program.
- **counterfactual**: The unobserved outcome that treatment units would have experienced without the program.
- **time-varying differences**: Changes affecting treatment and comparison groups differently over time, which can invalidate difference-in-differences estimates.

## Chapter Structure

- Before-after logic.
- Treatment and comparison changes.
- Equal trends assumption.
- Threats.
- Implementation examples.

## Practical Implications

- Use DD only when a credible comparison trend can be defended.
- Look for pre-program outcome histories rather than one baseline point.
- Document concurrent changes that could break the parallel trends assumption.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What does Chapter 7 help decide?"]
  Central["Concept: Difference-in-Differences"]
  Concept1["Concept: difference-in-differences"]
  Concept2["Concept: parallel trends"]
  Concept3["Concept: comparison group"]
  Mechanism1["Design element: before-after logic"]
  Mechanism2["Design element: treatment and comparison changes"]
  Mechanism3["Design element: equal trends assumption"]
  Implication1["Practice implication: Use DD only when a credible comparison trend can be defended."]
  Implication2["Practice implication: Look for pre-program outcome histories rather than one baseline point."]
  Question["Open question: What has to be checked in the source records?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Central --> Concept3
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Concept3 --> Mechanism3
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2,Concept3 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2,Mechanism3 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Chapter 6: Regression Discontinuity Design](chapter-06-regression-discontinuity-design.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter 8: Matching](chapter-08-matching.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [What Is Impact Evaluation?](section-01-what-is-impact-evaluation.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Constructing a Theory of Change](section-02-constructing-theory-of-change.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Causal Inference and Counterfactuals](section-03-causal-inference-counterfactuals.qmd)

## Study Prompts

- What does Chapter 7 imply for selecting or defending an impact evaluation design?
- Which assumption in this chapter would be hardest to defend in a live policy setting?
- Which source record should be reopened before using this note in applied work?

## Source Records

- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:ch07-chapter-7-difference-in-differences:c0001`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:evaluating-a-program-when-the-rule-of-assignment-is-less-clear:c0001`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:the-equal-trends-assumption-in-difference-in-differences:c0001`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:the-equal-trends-assumption-in-difference-in-differences:c0002`

## References
